content.assessment: Compensatory investment without structural change can partially offset resource losses to feeder schools but, by this lens's own within-school sorting evidence, cannot address classroom-level SES sorting or the structural drivers of segregation (tracking, church growth, geographic clustering), meaning gains are likely to be real but bounded and potentially reversible by ongoing sorting dynamics.
opportunities: Could be paired with any structural proposal (T1-T3, T6) to address the compensatory-funding gap that structural reform alone does not resolve
strengths: Directly counters a documented resource-loss mechanism: feeder schools lose funding and peer effects when high performers depart, with no existing compensatory mechanism
Targets the large disadvantaged-student skill gap (12.1% vs 16.3% EU average) independent of track structure, a channel not touched by any structural proposal in this set
threats: Persistent SES-driven access asymmetry in selective tracks (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-02) continues to concentrate advantage regardless of compensatory spending elsewhere in the system
Roma segregation, distinct from and larger than general SES segregation, is not directly targeted by generic compensatory measures (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-06)
weaknesses: Structural segregation mechanisms (tracking age, free choice, church-school growth) remain fully intact, so funding may only partially offset ongoing sorting rather than reverse it (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-07)

Within-school classroom sorting by SES, which has significant effects on test-score inequality, would likely persist and could absorb school-level funding gains entirely if classroom composition is not also addressed (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-10)
